planting.note: Sow outdoors around the last spring frost; tolerates light frost and can also be sown in late summer for fall bloom.

directions: Direct sow seeds about a quarter inch deep once the soil is workable in spring, or start indoors a few weeks before the last frost. This cultivar produces bronzy-orange petals that fade to soft pink over a maroon-backed, yellow-centered flower. Deadhead regularly to keep the edible blooms coming and prevent early fade in summer heat. Petals are edible and make a colorful addition to salads or as a garnish. Calendula self-sows readily and thrives in cool weather.

planting.note: Sow after all danger of frost has passed and soil has warmed; zinnias resent cold, wet ground.

directions: Direct sow after the last frost about a quarter inch deep, or start indoors a few weeks early for an earlier show. This scabiosa-flowered blend yields 2 to 2.5 inch double, semi-double, and occasional single blooms in soft pastel shades where no two flowers are alike. Pinch young plants once to encourage branching and more stems for cutting. Cut or deadhead frequently to keep the plants productive all season. Provide good air circulation to reduce powdery mildew.

planting.note: Sow directly after the last frost; successive sowings every couple of weeks extend the bloom season.

directions: Direct sow about one inch deep after the last frost once soil has warmed. This branching, pollenless-style florist sunflower blooms roughly two months from sowing, bearing golden-yellow 4 to 6 inch flowers on tall, sturdy stems ideal for bouquets. Space plants closer for single-stem cut flowers or wider to encourage branching and more blooms. Its long vase life makes it a favorite for the backyard florist. Stake in windy sites to keep the tall stems upright.

planting.note: Start indoors about 6 weeks before the last frost, then transplant out once frost danger has passed.

directions: Start seeds indoors about six weeks before the last frost and transplant after the weather warms, or direct sow in warm regions. Also called Monarch of the Veldt, this heat- and drought-tolerant South African annual produces 3 to 4 inch daisy-like flowers above silver-tinged foliage from midsummer to fall. Grow in full sun and well-drained soil, and avoid overwatering as it prefers drier conditions. The blooms attract bees, birds, and butterflies. Flowers close at night and on cloudy days.
